text: The video shows the placement of three laparoscopic trocars: one in the left upper quadrant, one in the left lateral abdomen, and one in the left lower quadrant, as part of the treatment for a perforated gastric ulcer using a gram patch approach.

The surgeon inspects the abdomen, revealing a significant amount of enteric content, which is evacuated using a suction device. Attention is then directed to the upper abdomen, where a small perforation of a pyloric channel ulcer is identified just beneath the surface of the liver, with the liver not fully covering the perforation.

The surgeon uses a suction device to evacuate enteric content from the perforation and through an NG tube. A tongue of omentum is prepared for the gram patch. Two sutures are introduced into the abdomen, and a two-oh silk stitch on an SH needle is placed above the perforation. The omentum is positioned under the stitch to cover the perforation, and the suture is secured with a figure of eight slip knot, ensuring minimal tension to avoid tearing the inflamed gastric wall.

The surgeon places a two-o-vicryl stitch on an SH needle below the perforation, securing it over the tongue of omentum and tying it to the other side of the perforation. A 10mm JP drain is then positioned around the patched perforation to manage potential leakage or gastric outlet obstruction.
